You are working inside Conductor, a Mac app that lets the user run many coding agents in parallel.
Your work should take place in the /Users/marcus/conductor/workspaces/brain-v1/munich-v2 directory (unless otherwise directed), which has been set up for you to work in.
Each workspace has a .context directory (gitignored) where you can save files to collaborate with other agents.
The target branch for this workspace is main. Use this for actions like creating new PRs, bisecting, etc., unless you're told otherwise.

By default, the user will only see the last message that you send before stopping. Include all essential information in the last message. The intermediate messages will be collapsed and accessible by the user but not displayed by default.

If the user asks you to work on several unrelated tasks, you can suggest they start new workspaces.
Sometimes the user might send you a message they meant to send in a different workspace or a different chat. If something doesn't make sense in the context of your work, just ask. 
If the user asks for help with Conductor, you can ask them to go to "Help -> Send Feedback" to get in touch with our team.
</system_instruction>

Perfect! I now have all the information needed. Let me provide a comprehensive summary of my findings.

---

## Agent Management UI and API: Complete Exploration

Based on my thorough exploration of the codebase, here's a comprehensive overview of how agent management works end-to-end:

### 1. What the /agents Page Shows

**Location:** `/Users/marcus/conductor/workspaces/brain-v1/munich-v2/app/src/client/routes/agents-page.tsx`

The agents page displays a list of agents grouped by **runtime type**:
- **Brain Agents** — System-managed agents (read-only, cannot be deleted)
- **External Agents** — Custom agents via MCP proxy tokens  
- **Sandbox Agents** — Custom agents in managed sandbox environments

Each agent card shows:
- Name, description (optional), runtime badge, creation date
- Edit/Delete buttons for non-brain agents
- Delete requires confirmation by typing the agent name

### 2. Agent CRUD API Endpoints

**Location:** `/Users/marcus/conductor/workspaces/brain-v1/munich-v2/app/src/server/agents/agent-route.ts`

Routes are wired in `app/src/server/runtime/start-server.ts`:

| Endpoint | Method | Purpose |
|----------|--------|---------|
| `/api/workspaces/:workspaceId/agents` | GET | List all agents in workspace (grouped by runtime) |
| `/api/workspaces/:workspaceId/agents` | POST | Create new agent (external or sandbox) |
| `/api/workspaces/:workspaceId/agents/:agentId` | GET | Fetch agent detail with identity, authority scopes, sessions |
| `/api/workspaces/:workspaceId/agents/:agentId` | DELETE | Delete agent (requires confirmation name match) |
| `/api/workspaces/:workspaceId/agents/check-name` | GET | Validate agent name uniqueness in workspace |

All endpoints:
- Resolve identity from Better Auth session (not from headers)
- Resolve workspace from path and validate access via `member_of` relation
- Return 401 if no identity/session; 404 if workspace not found

### 3. Agent Storage in SurrealDB

**Schema Location:** `/Users/marcus/conductor/workspaces/brain-v1/munich-v2/schema/surreal-schema.surql` (lines 332-350+)

**Core Tables:**

```
agent (SCHEMAFULL)
├── id (string, PK)
├── runtime (enum: "brain" | "sandbox" | "external")
├── name (string)
├── description (option<string>)
├── model (option<string>) — LLM model identifier for agent
├── sandbox_config (option<object>) — Sandbox-specific config
│   ├── coding_agents (option<array<string>>)
│   ├── env_vars (option<array<{key, value}>)
│   ├── image (option<string>)
│   └── snapshot (option<string>)
├── managed_by (record<identity>) — Relation to identity
└── created_at (datetime)

identity (stores both human and agent identities)
├── id (string, PK)
├── name (string)
├── type (enum: "human" | "agent" | "system") 
├── role (option<string>)
├── workspace (record<workspace>)
└── identity_status (enum: "active" | ...)

Relations:
├── identity_agent (IN: identity, OUT: agent)
│   └── added_at (datetime)
├── member_of (IN: identity, OUT: workspace)
│   ├── role (option<string>) — e.g., "agent"
│   └── added_at (datetime)
└── authorized_to (IN: identity, OUT: authority_scope)
    ├── permission (enum: "auto" | "propose" | "blocked")
    └── created_at (datetime)
```

**Authority Scopes:** 11 configurable actions:
- `create_decision`, `confirm_decision`
- `create_task`, `complete_task`
- `create_observation`, `acknowledge_observation`, `resolve_observation`
- `create_question`, `create_suggestion`
- `create_intent`, `submit_intent`

### 4. Agent Creation Flow (End-to-End)

**Frontend:**
1. `/agents/new` route navigates to `AgentCreatePage`
2. User selects runtime (External or Sandbox)
3. User fills form:
   - Name (validated for uniqueness via GET `/agents/check-name?name=...`)
   - Description (optional)
   - Model (optional) — for agent-specific LLM
   - Authority scopes (radio grid: Auto/Propose/Blocked per action)
4. Form POSTs to `POST /api/workspaces/:workspaceId/agents`

**Backend (Atomic Transaction):**
In `app/src/server/agents/agent-queries.ts`, `createAgentTransaction()` does:

1. **Pre-validate** name uniqueness (409 conflict if taken)
2. **CREATE agent record** with runtime, name, model, sandbox_config, managed_by identity
3. **CREATE identity record** with type="agent", workspace binding
4. **RELATE** `identity -> identity_agent -> agent`
5. **RELATE** `identity -> member_of -> workspace` (role="agent")
6. **RELATE** `identity -> authorized_to -> authority_scope` (11 edges, one per action with permission from input)
7. **For external agents only:** Generate proxy token, hash it, store in `proxy_token` table with TTL

Returns:
```json
{
  "agent": { "id", "name", "runtime", "identity_id", "created_at", ... },
  "proxy_token": "brn_..." (external agents only)
}
```

The proxy token is displayed once in `ProxyTokenDialog` component and must be saved immediately.

### 5. Task-Agent Assignment UI

**The connection exists but is limited in scope:**

**Component:** `app/src/client/components/graph/AgentStatusSection.tsx`

Shows agent assignment **only for tasks** in states: `open`, `ready`, or `todo`

**UI Flow:**
1. User views a task detail page
2. `AgentStatusSection` renders "Assign Agent" button (if task is assignable)
3. Click → calls `assignAgent(workspaceId, taskId)` → POST to `/api/orchestrator/:workspaceId/assign`
4. On success: returns `agentSessionId` + streaming endpoint
5. UI enters "active" state showing:
   - Live agent status badge (Spawning, Working, Idle, Completed, Aborted, Error)
   - File change count
   - Live SSE stream from orchestrator
   - Review link when idle

**Important Limitation:** There is **no CRUD UI** for managing which agents are assigned to tasks. The assignment is:
- Ephemeral (agent sessions, not permanent task→agent relations)
- One-way from task → agent session (not bidirectional)
- Managed through the orchestrator system, not the agent CRUD endpoints

**Data Model:**
```
agent_session (created by orchestrator)
├── id (string)
├── agent (string) — agent name or ID
├── workspace (record<workspace>)
├── task_id (option<record<task>>) — which task is being worked on
├── started_at (datetime)
├── ended_at (option<datetime>)
├── orchestrator_status (enum: "spawning" | "active" | "idle" | "completed" | ...)
├── summary (option<string>)
├── files_changed_count (number)
├── stream_id (string)
└── (various progress tracking fields)
```

**Entity Detail View Integration:**
When fetching task detail via `GET /api/entities/search`, if the task has an active agent session:
- `EntityDetailResponse.agentSession` includes summary (session ID, status, files changed, started_at)
- This feeds into `AgentStatusSection` for display

### 6. Authority Scopes and Permissions

**Creation:** Set at agent creation time via `authority_scopes` input array
```json
{
  "authority_scopes": [
    { "action": "create_task", "permission": "auto" },
    { "action": "confirm_decision", "permission": "propose" },
    { "action": "submit_intent", "permission": "blocked" }
  ]
}
```

**Default:** If not specified, all 11 actions default to `"propose"`

**Storage:** 11 `authorized_to` relation edges in SurrealDB (identity → authority_scope)

**Permission Levels:**
- `"auto"` — Agent can execute immediately without approval
- `"propose"` — Agent proposes the action; human approves
- `"blocked"` — Agent cannot perform this action

**View:** Agent detail page (`/agents/:agentId`) shows a table of all 11 actions + current permissions

### 7. Key Files for Quick Navigation

**Client Routes:**
- `/agents` — List page: `app/src/client/routes/agents-page.tsx`
- `/agents/new` — Create page: `app/src/client/routes/agent-create-page.tsx`
- `/agents/:agentId` — Detail page: `app/src/client/routes/agent-detail-page.tsx`

**Server CRUD:**
- Route handlers: `app/src/server/agents/agent-route.ts`
- Query functions: `app/src/server/agents/agent-queries.ts`
- Type definitions: `app/src/server/agents/types.ts`

**Client Hooks:**
- List + grouping: `app/src/client/hooks/use-agents.ts`
- CRUD actions: `app/src/client/hooks/use-agent-actions.ts`
- Session streaming: `app/src/client/hooks/use-agent-session.ts`

**Task-Agent Assignment:**
- UI component: `app/src/client/components/graph/AgentStatusSection.tsx`
- Orchestrator API: `app/src/client/graph/orchestrator-api.ts`
- Entity detail response: `app/src/server/entities/entity-detail-response.ts`

**UI Components:**
- Agent card: `app/src/client/components/agent/agent-card.tsx`
- Authority scope form: `app/src/client/components/agent/authority-scope-form.tsx`
- Proxy token dialog: `app/src/client/components/agent/proxy-token-dialog.tsx`

### Summary

Brain has a **complete agent management system** with three tiers:
1. **CRUD UI** for agent lifecycle (create, list, detail, delete) — fully built out
2. **Authority scope management** — configurable per agent via radio buttons
3. **Task assignment** — ephemeral, one-way, managed by orchestrator (not CRUD-based)

The **missing piece** is permanent task↔agent assignment relationships. The system currently uses temporary `agent_session` records spawned by the orchestrator, not persistent task→agent edges in SurrealDB. This is by design — agents are task-scoped, not permanently assigned.

## Summary of Task Assignment in the Brain UI

Based on my thorough exploration of the codebase, here's how tasks are created and assigned to agents:

### 1. Task Creation in the UI

**Task Creation Paths:**
- **Via Chat (Primary)**: Users create tasks indirectly through the chat interface using the `create_work_item` tool, which the chat agent can invoke
- **Work Item Suggestions**: The PM agent suggests batches of work items (tasks, features, projects) which users can accept or dismiss via the `WorkItemSuggestionList` component
- **Direct API**: Tasks can be created via POST to `/api/workspaces/:id/work-items/accept` for accepting suggestions

**No Direct UI Form**: There is NO form-based UI in the client side for creating tasks directly. Task creation happens:
1. Through natural language in chat messages (extraction pipeline converts to tasks)
2. Through PM agent suggestions (via `suggest_work_items` tool)
3. Through programmatic tool calls (`create_work_item` tool)

### 2. Owner/Assignee Fields

**Task Schema Fields (from surreal-schema.surql, lines 116-144):**
- `owner: option<record<identity>>` - An optional identity record that owns the task
- `owner_name: option<string>` - The readable name of the owner

**Current State**: The `owner` field exists in the schema but **is NOT actively used for assignment in the UI**. It's visible in entity detail but:
- Not settable when creating tasks via the `create_work_item` tool
- Not settable when editing tasks via the `edit_work_item` tool
- Not displayed with any assignment UI in the entity detail panel
- Only shown as read-only metadata in `EntityDetailPanel.tsx` (lines 222-227)

### 3. Agents List Page

**Location**: `/app/src/client/routes/agents-page.tsx`

**What it does:**
- Lists all workspace agents grouped by runtime: Brain, External, Sandbox
- Shows agent metadata (name, description, runtime type, created date)
- Allows deletion of external/sandbox agents (Brain agents are read-only)
- Allows creation of new external or sandbox agents

**Agent Configuration Available**:
- Name, description, model override
- Authority scopes (what actions the agent can perform: auto, propose, or blocked)
- Proxy tokens for external agents
- Recent workspace sessions

**No Task Assignment UI**: The agents page does NOT allow assigning agents to tasks. It's purely for agent management.

### 4. Chat Agent Work Item Tools

**Three Key Tools** (`app/src/server/tools/`):

**a) `suggest_work_items.ts`**
- Input schema: Batch of proposed work items (kind, title, rationale, category, project, priority)
- Uses BM25 full-text search to detect duplicates/merges
- Returns three buckets:
  - `suggestions[]` - New items to create
  - `updated[]` - Existing items to merge with
  - `discarded[]` - Exact duplicates to skip
- **No agent assignment field**

**b) `create_work_item.ts`**
- Input schema: Single work item (kind, title, rationale, category, priority, project, feature)
- Creates task/feature/project records directly
- Fields supported: title, status, category, priority, workspace
- **No owner/assignee field**

**c) `edit_work_item.ts`**
- Input schema: Existing work item updates (title, status, category, priority, rationale)
- Editable fields: title, status, category, priority, description
- **No owner/assignee field**

### 5. PM Agent Integration

**Location**: `/app/src/server/agents/pm/`

The PM agent (`agent.ts`) is a `ToolLoopAgent` that:
- Orchestrates work item management
- Composes shared tools: search_entities, get_project_status, create_observation, create_suggestion, suggest_work_items, create_work_item, edit_work_item, move_items_to_project
- Returns structured JSON: `{ summary, suggestions[], updated[], discarded[], observations_created }`

**Rendering**: The chat page renders PM suggestions as `WorkItemSuggestionList` component blocks (lines 137-150 in chat-page.tsx), which include AcceptAll/DismissAll controls

### 6. Task Property Display

**In EntityDetailPanel.tsx** (the entity detail sidebar, lines 222-227):
- Shows `owner_name` as read-only metadata if present
- Shows editable `priority` dropdown for tasks/decisions/questions
- Shows status, category, confidence, created date
- Shows `AgentStatusSection` for tasks to show if an agent session is associated

### Key Finding: No Agent Assignment Field

**The current system does NOT support assigning tasks to agents through:**
- Chat tool parameters
- Work item suggestion fields
- Entity editing UI
- Direct database assignment

Tasks can only be:
1. Associated with an `agent_session` (which agent worked on this task in what session)
2. Have an `owner` (identity record) for human accountability
3. Have an `source_session` (which agent session created this task)

The workflow is task-centric, not agent-assignment-centric: agents work on tasks autonomously based on context and authority scopes, rather than being explicitly assigned to tasks.

content.text:
Perfect! Now I have a comprehensive picture. Let me create a final summary document with all my findings:

## Summary of Task Assignment in the Brain UI

Based on my thorough exploration of the codebase, here's how tasks are created and assigned to agents:

### 1. Task Creation in the UI

**Task Creation Paths:**
- **Via Chat (Primary)**: Users create tasks indirectly through the chat interface using the `create_work_item` tool, which the chat agent can invoke
- **Work Item Suggestions**: The PM agent suggests batches of work items (tasks, features, projects) which users can accept or dismiss via the `WorkItemSuggestionList` component
- **Direct API**: Tasks can be created via POST to `/api/workspaces/:id/work-items/accept` for accepting suggestions

**No Direct UI Form**: There is NO form-based UI in the client side for creating tasks directly. Task creation happens:
1. Through natural language in chat messages (extraction pipeline converts to tasks)
2. Through PM agent suggestions (via `suggest_work_items` tool)
3. Through programmatic tool calls (`create_work_item` tool)

### 2. Owner/Assignee Fields

**Task Schema Fields (from surreal-schema.surql, lines 116-144):**
- `owner: option<record<identity>>` - An optional identity record that owns the task
- `owner_name: option<string>` - The readable name of the owner

**Current State**: The `owner` field exists in the schema but **is NOT actively used for assignment in the UI**. It's visible in entity detail but:
- Not settable when creating tasks via the `create_work_item` tool
- Not settable when editing tasks via the `edit_work_item` tool
- Not displayed with any assignment UI in the entity detail panel
- Only shown as read-only metadata in `EntityDetailPanel.tsx` (lines 222-227)

### 3. Agents List Page

**Location**: `/app/src/client/routes/agents-page.tsx`

**What it does:**
- Lists all workspace agents grouped by runtime: Brain, External, Sandbox
- Shows agent metadata (name, description, runtime type, created date)
- Allows deletion of external/sandbox agents (Brain agents are read-only)
- Allows creation of new external or sandbox agents

**Agent Configuration Available**:
- Name, description, model override
- Authority scopes (what actions the agent can perform: auto, propose, or blocked)
- Proxy tokens for external agents
- Recent workspace sessions

**No Task Assignment UI**: The agents page does NOT allow assigning agents to tasks. It's purely for agent management.

### 4. Chat Agent Work Item Tools

**Three Key Tools** (`app/src/server/tools/`):

**a) `suggest_work_items.ts`**
- Input schema: Batch of proposed work items (kind, title, rationale, category, project, priority)
- Uses BM25 full-text search to detect duplicates/merges
- Returns three buckets:
  - `suggestions[]` - New items to create
  - `updated[]` - Existing items to merge with
  - `discarded[]` - Exact duplicates to skip
- **No agent assignment field**

**b) `create_work_item.ts`**
- Input schema: Single work item (kind, title, rationale, category, priority, project, feature)
- Creates task/feature/project records directly
- Fields supported: title, status, category, priority, workspace
- **No owner/assignee field**

**c) `edit_work_item.ts`**
- Input schema: Existing work item updates (title, status, category, priority, rationale)
- Editable fields: title, status, category, priority, description
- **No owner/assignee field**

### 5. PM Agent Integration

**Location**: `/app/src/server/agents/pm/`

The PM agent (`agent.ts`) is a `ToolLoopAgent` that:
- Orchestrates work item management
- Composes shared tools: search_entities, get_project_status, create_observation, create_suggestion, suggest_work_items, create_work_item, edit_work_item, move_items_to_project
- Returns structured JSON: `{ summary, suggestions[], updated[], discarded[], observations_created }`

**Rendering**: The chat page renders PM suggestions as `WorkItemSuggestionList` component blocks (lines 137-150 in chat-page.tsx), which include AcceptAll/DismissAll controls

### 6. Task Property Display

**In EntityDetailPanel.tsx** (the entity detail sidebar, lines 222-227):
- Shows `owner_name` as read-only metadata if present
- Shows editable `priority` dropdown for tasks/decisions/questions
- Shows status, category, confidence, created date
- Shows `AgentStatusSection` for tasks to show if an agent session is associated

### Key Finding: No Agent Assignment Field

**The current system does NOT support assigning tasks to agents through:**
- Chat tool parameters
- Work item suggestion fields
- Entity editing UI
- Direct database assignment

Tasks can only be:
1. Associated with an `agent_session` (which agent worked on this task in what session)
2. Have an `owner` (identity record) for human accountability
3. Have an `source_session` (which agent session created this task)

The workflow is task-centric, not agent-assignment-centric: agents work on tasks autonomously based on context and authority scopes, rather than being explicitly assigned to tasks.

---

### Key File Locations:
- Schema: `/schema/surreal-schema.surql` (lines 116-144 for task table)
- Chat agent tools: `/app/src/server/tools/` (create-work-item.ts, edit-work-item.ts, suggest-work-items.ts)
- PM agent: `/app/src/server/agents/pm/` (agent.ts, tools.ts, prompt.ts)
- Client components: `/app/src/client/components/graph/EntityDetailPanel.tsx`, `/app/src/client/routes/agents-page.tsx`
- Work item suggestion schema: `/app/src/shared/chat-component-definitions.ts` (workItemSuggestionPropsSchema)

content.thinking: Based on the exploration, I can now give the user a clear answer about how agent assignment works in the UI.

content.text:
Based on exploring the codebase, here's how it currently works:

## Current State: Task → Agent Assignment

The UI already has an **`AgentStatusSection`** component (`app/src/client/components/graph/AgentStatusSection.tsx`) that appears on task detail views. When a task is in `open`, `ready`, or `todo` status, it shows an **"Assign Agent"** button.

However, the current flow is:

1. User clicks "Assign Agent" on a task
2. It calls `POST /api/orchestrator/:workspaceId/assign` 
3. The orchestrator spawns an **agent session** and streams progress back via SSE
4. The UI shows live status (Spawning → Working → Idle → Completed), file changes, and a review link

## What's Missing

**There's no agent picker.** The assignment call doesn't let the user choose *which* agent to assign — it just dispatches to the orchestrator without an agent selection step. The user can't browse their created agents (Brain, External, Sandbox) and say "assign this task to Agent X."

This is the gap between:
- **Agent CRUD** (fully built — create, list, detail, delete, authority scopes) at `/agents`
- **Task assignment** (exists but has no agent selection UI)

## What Would Need to Change

To let users choose which agent handles a task:

1. **Agent picker UI** — When clicking "Assign Agent" on a task, show a dropdown/modal listing workspace agents (filtered by those with `create_task`/`complete_task` authority)
2. **API change** — `POST /api/orchestrator/:workspaceId/assign` needs to accept an `agentId` parameter
3. **Orchestrator routing** — The orchestrator needs to route the session to the selected agent (Brain agent prompt, external MCP proxy, or sandbox environment) based on the agent's `runtime` type

Would you like me to design or implement the agent picker for task assignment?

1→/**
     2→ * Brain Tool Definitions — Single source of truth for Brain-native MCP tools.
     3→ *
     4→ * Zod schemas are the canonical definitions. JSON Schema is derived via
     5→ * Zod v4's toJSONSchema(). Tool files in tools/*.ts should import from
     6→ * here rather than re-defining schemas.
     7→ *
     8→ * Pure core: no IO, no DB, no side effects.
     9→ */
    10→import { z } from "zod";
    11→import z4 from "zod/v4";
    12→import { ENTITY_CATEGORIES, ENTITY_PRIORITIES, SUGGESTION_CATEGORIES } from "../../shared/contracts";
    13→
    14→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
    15→// McpToolDefinition type (self-contained, no circular import)
    16→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
    17→
    18→export type McpToolDefinition = {
    19→  readonly name: string;
    20→  readonly description: string;
    21→  readonly inputSchema: Record<string, unknown>;
    22→};
    23→
    24→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
    25→// Zod → MCP definition helper
    26→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
    27→
    28→function defineTool(
    29→  name: string,
    30→  description: string,
    31→  schema: z.ZodType,
    32→): McpToolDefinition {
    33→  const jsonSchema = z4.toJSONSchema(schema);
    34→  // Strip $schema key — MCP protocol doesn't use it
    35→  const { $schema: _, ...inputSchema } = jsonSchema as Record<string, unknown>;
    36→  return { name, description, inputSchema };
    37→}
    38→
    39→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
    40→// Shared schema fragments
    41→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
    42→
    43→const projectFeatureContext = z.object({
    44→  project: z.string().optional(),
    45→  feature: z.string().optional(),
    46→}).optional().describe("Project/feature scope");
    47→
    48→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
    49→// Infrastructure tools
    50→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
    51→
    52→export const createIntentSchema = z.object({
    53→  goal: z.string().describe("What you want to accomplish with this tool"),
    54→  reasoning: z.string().describe("Why this tool is needed for the current task"),
    55→  action_spec: z.object({
    56→    provider: z.string().describe("The toolkit/provider name (e.g. github, brain)"),
    57→    action: z.string().describe("The tool action name"),
    58→    params: z.record(z.string(), z.unknown()).optional().describe("Parameters for the tool call"),
    59→  }),
    60→  evidence_refs: z.array(z.string()).optional().describe(
    61→    "Graph record references justifying this intent, in table:id format " +
    62→    "(e.g. \"decision:abc123\", \"task:def456\"). " +
    63→    "Valid entity types: decision, task, observation, feature, project, policy, objective, learning, git_commit. " +
    64→    "Evidence quality affects authorization routing — stronger evidence (confirmed decisions, completed tasks) " +
    65→    "reduces risk assessment. Workspaces with hard enforcement require evidence for non-trivial intents.",
    66→  ),
    67→});
    68→
    69→export const getContextSchema = z.object({
    70→  intent: z.string().describe("Description of what you are working on"),
    71→});
    72→
    73→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
    74→// Read tool schemas
    75→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
    76→
    77→export const searchEntitiesSchema = z.object({

    80→    .optional()
    81→    .describe("Optional filter by entity kinds"),
    82→  limit: z.number().int().min(1).max(25).default(10).describe("Maximum number of results"),
    83→});
    84→

   204→export const CREATE_INTENT_TOOL = defineTool(
   205→  "create_intent",
   206→  "Create an intent to request authorization for a gated tool. " +
   207→  "Use this when a tool you need is marked as [GATED]. " +
   208→  "Provide the goal, reasoning, and action_spec describing the tool you want to use. " +
   209→  "Include evidence_refs to submit graph references (decision, task, observation, feature, " +
   210→  "project, policy, objective, learning, git_commit) that justify your intent. " +
   211→  "Evidence quality determines authorization routing: confirmed decisions and completed tasks " +
   212→  "carry the most weight. Workspaces may operate under enforcement modes (bootstrap, soft, hard) " +
   213→  "where hard enforcement rejects intents without sufficient evidence before LLM evaluation.",
   214→  createIntentSchema,
   215→);
   216→
   217→export const GET_CONTEXT_TOOL = defineTool(
   218→  "get_context",
   219→  "Get project context from the Brain knowledge graph. " +
   220→  "Returns decisions, tasks, observations, and other entities relevant to your current work.",
   221→  getContextSchema,
   222→);
   223→
   224→export const BRAIN_INFRASTRUCTURE_TOOLS: readonly McpToolDefinition[] = [
   225→  CREATE_INTENT_TOOL,
   226→  GET_CONTEXT_TOOL,
   227→];
   228→
   229→// Read
   230→export const BRAIN_READ_TOOLS: readonly McpToolDefinition[] = [
   231→  defineTool("search_entities",
   232→    "Full-text search across the knowledge graph. Use for finding entities by keyword or topic.",
   233→    searchEntitiesSchema),
   234→  defineTool("list_workspace_entities",
   235→    "List workspace entities by kind. Use to answer questions about what entities exist.",
   236→    listWorkspaceEntitiesSchema),
   237→  defineTool("get_entity_detail",
   238→    "Get full details about a specific entity including relationships, provenance, and related entities.",
   239→    getEntityDetailSchema),
   240→  defineTool("get_project_status",
   241→    "Get the current status of a project including active tasks, recent decisions, open questions, and features.",
   242→    getProjectStatusSchema),
   243→  defineTool("get_conversation_history",
   244→    "Search past conversations for discussions about a topic and return relevant message excerpts with linked entities.",
   245→    getConversationHistorySchema),
   246→  defineTool("check_constraints",
   247→    "Check if a proposed action conflicts with existing decisions or constraints. Returns hard conflicts, soft tensions, supporting context, and proceed flag.",
   248→    checkConstraintsSchema),
   249→  defineTool("resolve_decision",
   250→    "Search for existing decisions matching a question. Returns resolved (high match) or unresolved with related context. Read-only — use create_provisional_decision to create new decisions.",
   251→    resolveDecisionSchema),
   252→];
   253→
   254→// Write
   255→export const BRAIN_WRITE_TOOLS: readonly McpToolDefinition[] = [
   256→  defineTool("create_provisional_decision",
   257→    "Create a provisional decision when no existing answer exists. Use after resolve_decision returns unresolved.",
   258→    createProvisionalDecisionSchema),
   259→  defineTool("create_question",
   260→    "Create a question entity for open questions that require a choice or pending decision.",
   261→    createQuestionSchema),
   262→  defineTool("create_observation",
   263→    "Create an observation — risks, conflicts, gaps, or notable facts. Use proactively when you notice cross-cutting concerns.",
   264→    createObservationSchema),
   265→  defineTool("create_suggestion",
   266→    "Create a suggestion — a proactive, actionable proposal with rationale for the user to consider.",
   267→    createSuggestionSchema),
   268→  defineTool("create_work_item",
   269→    "Create a task, feature, or project directly in the knowledge graph.",
   270→    createWorkItemSchema),
   271→  defineTool("edit_work_item",
   272→    "Edit an existing task/feature/project by id. Use for rename or metadata updates.",
   273→    editWorkItemSchema),
   274→  defineTool("move_items_to_project",
   275→    "Move existing features or tasks to a different project. Deletes the old project edge and creates a new one.",
   276→    moveItemsToProjectSchema),
   277→  defineTool("acknowledge_observation",
   278→    "Mark an observation as acknowledged — reviewed but still needs resolution.",
   279→    acknowledgeObservationSchema),
   280→  defineTool("resolve_observation",
   281→    "Resolve an observation — the concern has been addressed.",
   282→    resolveObservationSchema),
   283→  defineTool("suggest_work_items",
   284→    "Process a batch of proposed work items and return PM triage buckets: suggestions, updated, discarded.",
   285→    suggestWorkItemsSchema),
   286→];
   287→
   288→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
   289→// Name sets
   290→// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
   291→
   292→export const BRAIN_READ_TOOL_NAMES: ReadonlySet<string> = new Set(
   293→  BRAIN_READ_TOOLS.map((t) => t.name),
   294→);
   295→
   296→export const BRAIN_WRITE_TOOL_NAMES: ReadonlySet<string> = new Set(
   297→  BRAIN_WRITE_TOOLS.map((t) => t.name),
   298→);
   299→
   300→/** All brain tool names including infrastructure tools (get_context, create_intent). */
   301→export const ALL_BRAIN_TOOL_NAMES: ReadonlySet<string> = new Set([
   302→  ...BRAIN_READ_TOOL_NAMES,
   303→  ...BRAIN_WRITE_TOOL_NAMES,
   304→  "get_context",
   305→  "create_intent",
   306→]);
   307→

1→# Entities
     2→
     3→Entity search, detail retrieval, and work item acceptance — the read/write API for interacting with knowledge graph nodes.
     4→
     5→## The Problem
     6→
     7→The knowledge graph contains thousands of entities across 15+ types (tasks, decisions, projects, features, observations). Users need to find entities by text, view their relationships and provenance, and accept suggested work items into the graph. The search must be fast (BM25 full-text, not vector) for the UI, while the detail view must expose the full relationship web around any entity.
     8→
     9→## What It Does
    10→
    11→- **BM25 full-text search**: Per-entity-type SQL queries with literal string interpolation (SurrealDB limitation — `@N@` doesn't work with bound parameters)
    12→- **Entity detail**: Loads an entity with all relationships, provenance chains, and neighboring nodes
    13→- **Work item acceptance**: Converts PM agent suggestions into actual task/feature/project records with embeddings and project linking
    14→- **Action endpoints**: Entity-specific mutations (confirm decision, acknowledge observation, etc.)
    15→
    16→## Key Concepts
    17→
    18→| Term | Definition |
    19→|------|------------|
    20→| **BM25 Search** | SurrealDB's built-in full-text search with snowball stemming — used for UI search, not semantic |
    21→| **Entity Detail** | Full entity record + incoming/outgoing edges + provenance + neighbors |
    22→| **Work Item Accept** | Flow: PM suggests → user accepts → task/feature created in graph with embedding |
    23→| **Fulltext Index** | Per-field `FULLTEXT ANALYZER` index with `BM25(1.2, 0.75)` scoring |
    24→
    25→## How It Works
    26→
    27→**Search flow:**
    28→
    29→1. User types "rate limiting" in search bar
    30→2. `GET /api/entities/search?q=rate+limiting&workspace=...`
    31→3. Handler escapes single quotes, builds per-type SQL with `@1@` match operator
    32→4. SurrealDB returns BM25-scored results across task, decision, feature, project, observation tables
    33→5. Results ranked by `search::score(1)`, deduplicated, returned to UI
    34→
    35→**Work item acceptance:**
    36→
    37→1. PM agent suggests: `{ type: "task", title: "Migrate billing to tRPC", project: "infra" }`
    38→2. User clicks "Accept" in chat UI
    39→3. `POST /api/workspaces/:id/work-items/accept` with suggestion payload
    40→4. Handler creates `task` record, generates embedding, links to project via `belongs_to` edge
    41→5. Returns created entity ID for UI navigation
    42→
    43→## Where It Fits
    44→
    45→```text
    46→UI Search Bar                PM Agent Suggestions
    47→  |                              |
    48→  v                              v
    49→GET /entities/search        POST /work-items/accept
    50→  |                              |
    51→  v                              v
    52→BM25 fulltext queries       Create task/feature record
    53→  |                           + embedding
    54→  v                           + belongs_to edge
    55→Scored results                   |
    56→                                 v
    57→                            Created entity in graph
    58→```
    59→
    60→**Consumes**: Search queries, work item suggestions from PM agent
    61→**Produces**: Search results, entity detail views, created graph nodes
    62→
    63→## File Structure
    64→
    65→```text
    66→entities/
    67→  entity-search-route.ts       # BM25 full-text search with per-type SQL generation
    68→  entity-detail-route.ts       # Entity detail with relationships and provenance
    69→  entity-action-route.ts       # Entity-specific mutations (confirm, acknowledge, etc.)
    70→  work-item-accept-route.ts    # Convert PM suggestions into graph records
    71→```
    72→

1→# Agents
     2→
     3→Specialized AI subagents that the chat orchestrator dispatches to handle domain-specific tasks — product management, analytics, and continuous graph verification.
     4→
     5→## The Problem
     6→
     7→A single chat agent cannot be an expert at everything. When a user asks "What's the status of project X?", the chat agent needs PM expertise. When they ask "How many tasks were completed last week?", it needs analytics skills. When the system needs to verify that a completed task actually matches its acceptance criteria, it needs an autonomous verifier. Cramming all of this into one prompt produces mediocre results across every domain.
     8→
     9→## What It Does
    10→
    11→- **PM Agent**: Manages work items — creates tasks/features, suggests work item batches with deduplication, checks project status, and organizes backlogs
    12→- **Analytics Agent**: Answers data and business questions by generating SurrealQL queries against the knowledge graph, with few-shot examples and syntax reference
    13→- **Observer Agent**: Autonomously verifies graph state — checks task completion claims, confirms decision implementations, and peer-reviews other observations
    14→
    15→## Key Concepts
    16→
    17→| Term | Definition |
    18→|------|------------|
    19→| **ToolLoopAgent** | Vercel AI SDK pattern where an agent iterates tool calls until it produces a final structured response |
    20→| **Subagent Dispatch** | Chat agent invokes a subagent via tool call (e.g. `invoke_pm_agent`), delegating a scoped task |
    21→| **Intent** | The reason for dispatching — `plan_work`, `check_status`, `organize`, `track_dependencies` (PM) or a natural language question (Analytics) |
    22→| **Graph Coordination** | Agents write observations/suggestions to the graph, never to each other directly |
    23→| **Verification Pipeline** | Observer checks claims against actual state via LLM reasoning with confidence scoring |
    24→
    25→## How It Works
    26→
    27→**PM Agent — work item triage:**
    28→
    29→1. Chat agent calls `invoke_pm_agent` with intent `plan_work` and user context
    30→2. PM agent loads workspace projects and open observations
    31→3. Uses tools: `search_entities`, `get_project_status`, `suggest_work_items`, `create_work_item`, `create_observation`
    32→4. Returns structured JSON: `{ summary, suggestions, updated, discarded, observations_created }`
    33→5. Chat agent renders suggestions as `WorkItemSuggestionList` component blocks
    34→
    35→**Analytics Agent — data question answering:**
    36→
    37→1. Chat agent calls `invoke_analytics_agent` with the user's question
    38→2. Analytics agent receives SurrealQL syntax reference and few-shot examples in its system prompt
    39→3. Generates and executes SurrealQL queries against the workspace graph
    40→4. Formats results as natural language answers with supporting data
    41→
    42→**Observer Agent — state verification:**
    43→
    44→1. Observer scan triggers verification of graph claims (e.g. "task X is complete")
    45→2. Observer agent loads the claim and supporting evidence from the graph
    46→3. Uses LLM reasoning to verify claim against evidence, producing confidence score
    47→4. Peer review cross-validates findings to prevent false positives
    48→5. Writes observations back to graph for human review
    49→
    50→## Where It Fits
    51→
    52→```text
    53→User Message
    54→  |
    55→  v
    56→Chat Agent (Sonnet, thin orchestrator)
    57→  |
    58→  +---> invoke_pm_agent ---------> PM Agent (Haiku)
    59→  |                                  +-> search_entities
    60→  |                                  +-> suggest_work_items
    61→  |                                  +-> create_observation
    62→  |                                  +-> structured JSON output
    63→  |
    64→  +---> invoke_analytics_agent ---> Analytics Agent (Haiku)
    65→  |                                  +-> execute SurrealQL
    66→  |                                  +-> format results
    67→  |
    68→  +---> (triggered by Observer scan)
    69→                                    Observer Agent (Haiku)
    70→                                     +-> verify task completion
    71→                                     +-> confirm decision implementation
    72→                                     +-> peer review observations
    73→                                     +-> write findings to graph
    74→```
    75→
    76→**Consumes**: User intents via chat agent dispatch, graph state for context loading
    77→**Produces**: Work item suggestions, analytics answers, verification observations
    78→
    79→## File Structure
    80→
    81→```text
    82→agents/
    83→  analytics/
    84→    agent.ts            # ToolLoopAgent setup, query execution, result formatting
    85→    few-shot-examples.ts # Example SurrealQL queries for in-context learning
    86→    prompt.ts           # System prompt with workspace context injection
    87→    syntax-reference.ts # SurrealQL syntax guide for query generation
    88→    tools.ts            # Analytics-specific tools (execute_query)
    89→  observer/
    90→    agent.ts            # Verification agent with claim checking tools
    91→    prompt.ts           # Observer system prompt with verification instructions
    92→    tools.ts            # Observer tools (verify_claim, peer_review)
    93→  pm/
    94→    agent.ts            # PM ToolLoopAgent with structured JSON output schema
    95→    prompt.ts           # PM system prompt with project/observation context
    96→    tools.ts            # Composed shared tools (search, suggest, create)
    97→```
    98→

data.command:
Review this conversation for items that were discussed but NOT logged via the Brain MCP tools.

IMPORTANT: If this was a trivial session (greetings, test messages, simple Q&A with no implementation work), immediately respond with:
{"decision":"approve","summary":"No substantive work to log"}
Do NOT explain why there is nothing to log.

Otherwise, check each category:

1. **Decisions**: Architecture or design choices where alternatives existed (e.g. choosing a pattern, library, data model, or trade-off). Do NOT log mechanical bugfixes, typo corrections, or changes with only one correct approach. Call create_provisional_decision for each genuine decision.
2. **Questions**: Unresolved questions raised but not logged. Call ask_question for each.
3. **Observations**: Contradictions, duplication, missing items, deprecated patterns, or anomalies noticed. Call log_observation for each.
4. **Task progress**: Tasks worked on whose status wasn't updated. Call update_task_status for each.
5. **Subtasks identified**: Work items or follow-up tasks discussed but not created. Call create_subtask for each (requires a parent task).
6. **Suggestions**: Optimizations, risks, opportunities, missing elements, or potential pivots noticed during work. Call create_suggestion for each with category (optimization|risk|opportunity|conflict|missing|pivot), rationale, and confidence (0-1).

After logging everything, respond with valid JSON in this exact shape:
{"decision":"approve","summary":"<one-line session summary>","decisions_made":["<decision-id>"],"questions_asked":["<question-id>"],"tasks_progressed":[{"task_id":"<task-id>","from_status":"<from>","to_status":"<to>"}],"files_changed":[{"path":"<path>","change_type":"created|modified|deleted"}],"observations_logged":["<observation-id>"],"subtasks_created":["<subtask-id>"],"suggestions_created":["<suggestion-id>"]}

If you cannot log required items (for example MCP unavailable), respond with:
{"decision":"block","reason":"Log these items first: ..."}

6. **Suggestions**: Optimizations, risks, opportunities, missing elements, or potential pivots noticed during work. Call create_suggestion for each with category (optimization|risk|opportunity|conflict|missing|pivot), rationale, and confidence (0-1).

After logging everything, respond with valid JSON in this exact shape:
{"decision":"approve","summary":"<one-line session summary>","decisions_made":["<decision-id>"],"questions_asked":["<question-id>"],"tasks_progressed":[{"task_id":"<task-id>","from_status":"<from>","to_status":"<to>"}],"files_changed":[{"path":"<path>","change_type":"created|modified|deleted"}],"observations_logged":["<observation-id>"],"subtasks_created":["<subtask-id>"],"suggestions_created":["<suggestion-id>"]}

If you cannot log required items (for example MCP unavailable), respond with:
{"decision":"block","reason":"Log these items first: ..."}

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /Users/marcus/.claude/skills/nw-design

# NW-DESIGN: Architecture Design

**Wave**: DESIGN (wave 3 of 6) | **Agents**: Morgan (nw-solution-architect) | **Command**: `*design-architecture`

## Overview

Execute DESIGN wave through discovery-driven architecture design. Morgan asks about business drivers and constraints first, then recommends architecture that fits. Analyzes existing codebase, evaluates open-source alternatives, produces C4 diagrams (Mermaid) as mandatory output.

## Prior Wave Consultation

Before beginning DESIGN work, read targeted prior wave artifacts:

1. **DISCOVER** (synthesis check only): Read `docs/feature/{feature-id}/discover/wave-decisions.md` — if any decision is unclear or relevant to architecture, read the referenced source file
2. **DISCUSS** (primary input): Read these key artifacts from `docs/feature/{feature-id}/discuss/`:
   - `wave-decisions.md` — decision summary
   - `requirements.md` — functional requirements
   - `acceptance-criteria.md` — testable criteria driving architecture
   - `user-stories.md` — scope of what to build
   - `story-map.md` — walking skeleton and release slicing
   - `outcome-kpis.md` — quality attributes informing architecture

DISCUSS already synthesizes DISCOVER evidence into structured requirements. DESIGN does not need raw DISCOVER artifacts (problem-validation, interview-log, etc.) unless wave-decisions.md flags something architecturally significant.

**READING ENFORCEMENT**: You MUST read every file listed in Prior Wave Consultation above using the Read tool before proceeding. After reading, output a confirmation checklist (`✓ {file}` for each read, `⊘ {file} (not found)` for missing). Do NOT skip files that exist — skipping causes architectural decisions disconnected from requirements.

After reading, check whether any DESIGN decisions would contradict DISCUSS requirements. Flag contradictions and resolve with user before proceeding. Example: DISCUSS requires "real-time updates" but DESIGN chooses batch processing — this must be resolved.

## Document Update (Back-Propagation)

When DESIGN decisions change assumptions from prior waves:
1. Document the change in a `## Changed Assumptions` section at the end of the affected DESIGN artifact
2. Reference the original prior-wave document and quote the original assumption
3. State the new assumption and the rationale for the change
4. If architecture constraints require changes to user stories or acceptance criteria, note them in `docs/feature/{feature-id}/design/upstream-changes.md` for the product owner to review

## Discovery Flow

Architecture decisions driven by quality attributes, not pattern shopping:

### Step 1: Understand the Problem
Review JTBD artifacts from DISCUSS to understand which jobs the architecture must serve. Morgan asks: What are we building? For whom? Which quality attributes matter most? (scalability|maintainability|testability|time-to-market|fault tolerance|auditability)

### Step 2: Understand Constraints
Morgan asks: Team size/experience? Timeline? Existing systems to integrate? Regulatory requirements? Operational maturity (CI/CD, monitoring)?

### Step 3: Team Structure (Conway's Law)
Morgan asks: How many teams? Communication patterns? Does proposed architecture match org chart?

### Step 3.5: Development Paradigm Selection

Morgan identifies primary language(s) from constraints, then applies:

- **FP-native** (Haskell|F#|Scala|Clojure|Elixir): recommend Functional
- **OOP-native** (Java|C#|Go): recommend OOP
- **Multi-paradigm** (TypeScript|Kotlin|Python|Rust|Swift): present both, let user choose based on team experience and domain fit

After confirmation, ask user permission to write paradigm to project CLAUDE.md:
- FP: `This project follows the **functional programming** paradigm. Use @nw-functional-software-crafter for implementation.`
- OOP: `This project follows the **object-oriented** paradigm. Use @nw-software-crafter for implementation.`

Default if user declines/unsure: OOP. User can override later.

### Step 4: Recommend Architecture Based on Drivers
Recommend based on quality attribute priorities|constraints|paradigm from Steps 1-3.5. Default: modular monolith with dependency inversion (ports-and-adapters). Overrides require evidence.

If functional paradigm selected, Morgan adapts architectural strategy:
- Types-first design: define algebraic data types and domain models before components
- Composition pipelines: data flows through transformation chains, not method dispatch
- Pure core / effect shell: domain logic is pure, IO lives at boundaries (adapters are functions)
- Effect boundaries replace port interfaces: function signatures serve as ports
- Immutable state: state changes produce new values, no mutation in the domain
These are strategic guidance items for the architecture document — no code snippets.

### Step 5: Advanced Architecture Stress Analysis (HIDDEN -- `--residuality` flag only)
When activated: apply complexity-science-based stress analysis — stressors|attractors|residues|incidence matrix|resilience modifications. See `stress-analysis` skill.
When not activated: skip entirely, do not mention.

### Step 6: Produce Deliverables
- Architecture document with component boundaries|tech stack|integration patterns
- C4 System Context diagram (Mermaid) -- MANDATORY
- C4 Container diagram (Mermaid) -- MANDATORY
- C4 Component diagrams (Mermaid) -- only for complex subsystems
- ADRs for significant decisions

## Rigor Profile Integration

Before dispatching the architect agent, read rigor config from `.nwave/des-config.json` (key: `rigor`). If absent, use standard defaults.

- **`agent_model`**: Pass as `model` parameter to Task tool. If `"inherit"`, omit `model` (inherits from session).
- **`reviewer_model`**: If design review is performed, use this model for the reviewer agent. If `"skip"`, skip design review.
- **`review_enabled`**: If `false`, skip post-design review step.

## Agent Invocation

@nw-solution-architect

Execute \*design-architecture for {feature-id}.

Context files: see Prior Wave Consultation above.

**Configuration:**
- model: rigor.agent_model (omit if "inherit")
- interactive: moderate
- output_format: markdown
- diagram_format: mermaid (C4)
- stress_analysis: {true if --residuality flag, false otherwise}

**SKILL_LOADING**: Read your skill files at `~/.claude/skills/nw-{skill-name}/SKILL.md`. At Phase 4, always load: `nw-architecture-patterns`, `nw-architectural-styles-tradeoffs`. Do NOT load `nw-roadmap-design` during DESIGN wave -- roadmap creation belongs to the DELIVER wave (`/nw-roadmap` or `/nw-deliver`). Then follow your Skill Loading Strategy table for phase-specific skills.

## Success Criteria

- [ ] Business drivers and constraints gathered before architecture selection
- [ ] Existing system analyzed before design (codebase search performed)
- [ ] Integration points with existing components documented
- [ ] Reuse vs. new component decisions justified
- [ ] Architecture supports all business requirements
- [ ] Technology stack selected with clear rationale
- [ ] Development paradigm selected and (optionally) written to project CLAUDE.md
- [ ] Component boundaries defined with dependency-inversion compliance
- [ ] C4 System Context + Container diagrams produced (Mermaid)
- [ ] ADRs written with alternatives considered
- [ ] Handoff accepted by nw-platform-architect (DEVOPS wave)

## Next Wave

**Handoff To**: nw-platform-architect (DEVOPS wave)
**Deliverables**: See Morgan's handoff package specification in agent file

## Wave Decisions Summary

Before completing DESIGN, produce `docs/feature/{feature-id}/design/wave-decisions.md`:

```markdown
# DESIGN Decisions — {feature-id}

## Key Decisions
- [D1] {decision}: {rationale} (see: {source-file})

## Architecture Summary
- Pattern: {e.g., modular monolith with ports-and-adapters}
- Paradigm: {OOP|FP}
- Key components: {list top-level components}

## Technology Stack
- {language/framework}: {rationale}

## Constraints Established
- {architectural constraint}

## Upstream Changes
- {any DISCUSS assumptions changed, with rationale}
```

This summary enables DEVOPS and DISTILL to quickly assess architecture decisions without reading all DESIGN files.

## Expected Outputs

```
docs/feature/{feature-id}/design/
  architecture-design.md       (includes C4 diagrams in Mermaid)
  technology-stack.md
  component-boundaries.md
  data-models.md
  wave-decisions.md
docs/adrs/
  ADR-NNN-*.md
CLAUDE.md (project root)   (optional: ## Development Paradigm section)
```
